speaker: interviewer
text: --her maiden name was Wos. You were talking about the kinds of food that you had in Flossenburg and the bread-- size of the bread. Did you have anything else to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Each day we received piece of bread, about 200, as I said. Every second day, piece of margarine, which was maybe 2 millimeters thick, and 1 by 1 inch big. Once a week, one teaspoon of sugar. Every day, one cup of very thin soup. If you find-- found one piece of potato, you, you were [LAUGHS] very happy, but mostly from leaves of beets. And one time, ersatz soup-- ersatz coffee-- you know what that is, 'ersatz.' And that's it. That was all. But this-- I was so happy, unfortunately, that my, my mother divide own portion for three. She gave us one third of portion, therefore, maybe I'm-- I could survive. But she lost lot of weight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from Flossenburg to Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, from Flossenburg, they took us again with this cattle train. And we came to, to Ravensbruck-- the same grating with Arbeit, macht macht frei. But this was very, very big campus. First, they took us to shower place, where they had-- they told us to strip everything. The first day were looking for jewelry, which some women were hidden in their hair or gynecological place. Each woman was taken first to check hair. One SS woman who checked my hair, she said, you bastard, you have too nice hair. And she shaved me. I was shocked. It was big loss for me. And later, to gynecological examination, which was more difficult for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was virgin. And I can't understand how they could-- in private places. But they didn't argue-- not only-- we had to do what they want. Later, we, we were taken to shower with cold water, of course, with some kind of disinfectant. And later, they gave us another dresses, also with stripe on the back. And nothing much-- nothing was proper, nothing warm. And they let us wait on the open place, which was, unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] upper part of crematorium. All the time, 24 hours daily, they burnt corpses. And smell was unbelievable, so hard to-- even to be there. After a couple days, they took us to one barrack. They check us-- they count us each day at 5 o'clock in the-- when still was dark each day, and keeping us in Appellplatz for a couple hours. One day, I lost strength, and I was falling down. I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ran very high temperature. This SS woman-- you know how they had big, black leather shoes-- and with whip inside. She took this whip, she beat me, and said in nice words, you have to stay, you. Then other women, they tried to keep me for counting, from other-- each place taking with finger because I-- I-- I was too, too weak. Thank God, after a couple days, they took us to Appellplatz. Somebody thought that this transportation of women-- this was only for women, Ravensbruck-- it will be-- is, is going to ammunition factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody has to strip. I remember cold, because it was autumn. And in the middle was sitting on the chair, a couple civilian men and couple SS man, SS man officers. And they, they let us go around in circle, naked. Again, shock for me to be with old women, with my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you seen your mother naked before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. It was a different time, not, not like now. Mother was, for me, always very elegant, very clean, very nice person. And later, I saw her naked-- shocked. And many women wounded or with some diseases. It was also terrible. And when we ran-- not walking, running-- around this circle, this SS man and this-- in the farming industry man, they show only by finger for girls. They took me, my sister, and all young women. Then other women were taken to other side to stay in Ravensbruck. In this moment, my mother broke. And she fall down on the knee close to this SS man, begged him, take me-- in German language-- take me with my daughters. I don't know how and why-- he agreed. And thank God for us because maybe she helped us survive. Then they took us again for this transport to Spandau. Sachsenhausen was main, main campus. But Spandau was small, very close to Berlin, 7, 7 kilometer only from Berlin. And they brought us in the evening, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were, were waiting for us in black uniform. This-- I don't know, SS or somebody-- some other animals with big German shepherd, with carabines, with everything, with whips. Quick! Raus, raus, raus! They took us to campus, where it was, of course, wire on high voltage. A big, big building-- it was like place where before was wagons, trams-- wagons or something. Because still, we saw that this place was something else and covered with very thin [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] roof. Inside was three bunks of beds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Infirmatory was very small for three beds-- infirmary, [LAUGHS] sorry. And everybody took place. One cover was again some, some-- no dresses. Only we received-- we took what we brought from Ravensbruck. And very soon, they took us to ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back for a minute, to how you described the crematoriums. Did you know who was going into the crematoriums while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we know. I heard in the same-- in Ravensbruck, my aunt, and my cousin, very close cousin-- but I couldn't find them. But anybody knew that they burning corpse of women who died or was killed, or after experimental medical experience. They burned this. And we know that is 24 hours. But we can't go there to see, of course. But the smell was terrible-- together, hair smelling, body smelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a smell was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I, I can't even to tell you. If you-- did you burn a little hair? Never? Wool-- something like mixture, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your internments in Flossenburg and Ravensbruck and then in Spandau, were you mixed in with Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. Because we, we were separated. In Flossenburg, without men. In Ravensbruck, only our group-- probably in our group who were Jewish women. But nobody knows. Nobody-- you are Jewish. You are Catholic. No-- together. But mostly, I met a Jewish woman in Spandau in-- close to Berlin. I had a very bad experience, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I helped this Jewish woman, and I was beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I have to tell you first-- we were working in this factory, a factory for ammunition. Because of bombarding, there was closed window and not too much light. And we were working on machine, which made ammunition for, for some-- I don't remember-- so big, for weapons. And I heard that on other machine is working-- was working Jewish woman who lost life because her hair was to transmission taken. And she was melted in this. It was shocking for us. But we were together. We, we never saw that this is Jewish, this is Catholic, no. Only we kept with my mother and these two girls, together. Because we, we were very close. And also after that-- after this work, I got-- around, around Christmastime-- I run very high fever. I was sick. They took me to infirmary. They treat me with prontosil. In this time, it was very popular. And one day, when I was almost OK, came Aufseherrinen-- [INAUDIBLE], this mean SS woman. And she asked me something. I, answer in German, in proper German. She said, OK, go with me. You will not go to ammunition factory. Go with me to work in warehouse, where they kept food for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all people and for SS group. I suppose that will be better. No-- [LAUGHS] 16 hours daily. I am like skeleton, like shadow, working so hard to clean this barrel, to, to clean floor. But I had some possibility to steal something. My highlight, my goal, was to bring in small pocketbook to my mother and my sister. But I-- slowly I stop to talk because I was depressed, going without hair, without dress, without underwear. I was passing urine like crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was still in factory-- and they took us at 5 o'clock in the morning with a shepherd-- German shepherd dogs. We were-- we were going about couple kilometers to this factory. But everybody wants to be on the front. Because when was on the end, the German shepherd was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] biting people on the end. I remember that my mother was so, so weak. We had to help her and to carry her, almost. In this time, it was cold, wintertime. And I had frostbite on my leg here. It was open wound-- pus going out because was open. And also passing, without knowing, urine. I tried to put some rags or something, but it doesn't help. This time was very hard for me-- losing weight. I remember one day, when was hurry to go to factory, I was not enough fast. This commandant of our Kommando said order to German Shepherd to get her. And he jumped on me. He threw me on the floor. And he start to bite me. I covered face. But he opened wound on the right side. It was very painful, very bleeding. They didn't let me go to make some cover. I was bleeding, very weak. You know, this wound was not, not, not clean. This was very bad. And after this dog opened my body, he called him up. It was very bad. [SOBS] And returning to this time when this SS woman told me to work in warehouse, it was a little, a little better. 16 hours for me, it was, very tragically, a long time. But this highlight-- mother, give her something. And you know, one day, I was unloading, I remember that-- everything what, what came to this campus, I had-- I had to unload. For example, it was old truck with 30 kilo of salt. It is about 20 pounds salt I had to carry to this warehouse on my back. I don't know how, but I did it. And one day, also, I unload bread. I was standing on the table, through the window, taking from this man bread and putting on the shelf. In this time was passing group of women, not my language. Some-- I knew that they are from Hungary, Jewish women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- they, they said. Somebody said me they Jewish from Hungary. They were living next barrack. And she-- only showing me [INAUDIBLE] that to give her something to eat. And I didn't know about the consequences. I put small piece. And I throw her, supposing that nobody see. Putting this loaf of bread different way, nobody can see. But in this moment, behind me, was watching me SS woman, young, beautiful girl with green uniform-- big shoes, black, with whip. She came to me. And she start to beat me-- terrible. She beat me first with fist, with whip. And later, when I fall down, she kick me in my cheek. I lost two teeth. I spill out-- and full cheek of blood. I was blue and black when this day I returned to my block. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But if somebody asked me, do that, if you help, I will. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's life. You see, because I believe if you do something good for other people, it'll return to you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, it was the worst experience for me. But [LAUGHS] I was not lucky. In this moment, she was watching me behind me. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it last till end, end of April. I think that about beginning of May or end of April, they took us from this campus. And, and there, of course, was soldier and ammunition. They took us to the west because they afraid. Because front army, Russian Army, were approach closer and closer to Berlin. They took us to this march to the west. First, I remember it was very cold. And these wooden shoes and these clothes not proper. They, they gave us small piece of bread. And they took us. First day, we march 90-- 49 kilometers. It is about 16 miles-- no, 30 miles. You're young, you can-- [LAUGHS] yes, about 30 kilometers-- miles, miles during rain, very cold weather. And many, many soldier and civils going to the west. Everybody was afraid-- not American, only Russian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many women went?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all, all women from this campus, from this barrack-- all women. Between them, it was Russian, also; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish, I am sure, also; and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pole, I don't remember; oh, and French, also. I remember one French woman all the time preparing hair nice. [LAUGHS] You see-- and then they put us, for a couple days, to the west. And we, we hoped that we can joined American army. They can liberate us. But we are-- we were very unhappy. One day during this journey, I fall down to water. I was completely frozen. And sitting in this position, start to have again a big-- very high temperature and to be yellow with jaundice. They put me close to fire to a little make me warmer. Still, I was very young and very fragile. Maybe I was 30 kilo, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still without hair, maybe [POINTS] so, so high. And we saw that we are liberated by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Russian. It was devastating. Because they start immediately to drink, to rape, to kill. [SIGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the Germans at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, German soldier in uniform went as much as possible-- as far as possible to the west. Private civilian people, they were going also to the west. But not everybody could go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of you when you were marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: For first two or three days, SS women and men were with us. But when they saw that it's not enough fast going to west, they left us one night. They took a supply of-- first of bread. They steal everything. And they left us alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We feel that we, we can survive. But we are between two armies. And they were fighting, and they-- shooting. And, you see, you never know if you can survive. Because they going there. They coming here. Nobody knows this is Polish or German. And they could kill us. And it was a really very, very bad moment because we, we were between in, in the place between two armies.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first time that you saw the Russians coming to liberate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw in small-- in small village close after Schwerin. Schwerin is on the north of Berlin, close to Hamburg. We were walking so much. But later, when we saw that they liberated us, it was no way to go to the west. We have to decide to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, we liberated you. We're friend. They said so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you nervous about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, because we, we saw this-- they-- not civilized person, like animal, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] drunk like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] difficult animals, and start to rape all women, even after delivering small babies. They raped all over, one after other. Can you imagine this animal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my experience, yes. When they saw that I'm very sick with mother and the other girls, they said, come on to our-- to this room. We give you food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother said no, I can't go, because she's very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one very dark-hair man, like [NON-ENGLISH], like something terrible, he said, what's the name of this girl? Wanda. Wanda, come, come with me. And he was crazy about taking me to his place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he start to-- it, it start to be cold-- dark. And he came to me. If not, I shot you. People start to explain to him, she's sick. Mother begged him. And I was really in very bad condition. They took me to barn, to put between other women on the floor. He came with other man. He was completely drunk. He took pistol, and he want to shot me. You see, I was standing in this moment, when he came. I don't know how and who-- why, I fall down to the ground. And I was crawling, still with very high temperature. Mother was asking, begging him. And I-- in the corner was standing Percheron, a, a horse, you see, very, very big horse. And probably because he was afraid he'd fall down, he was lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick this up on the next tape. Thank--
